stem: A sequence is defined by \( x_1 = 1 \) and \( x_{n+1} = \sqrt{[[a]] + [[b]]\,x_n} \) for \( n \ge 1 \). It converges to a positive limit \( L \) satisfying \( L = \sqrt{[[a]] + [[b]]L} \). What is \( L \)?
solution: Squaring \( L = \sqrt{[[a]] + [[b]]L} \) gives \( L^2 - [[b]]L - [[a]] = 0 \), so \( L = \dfrac{[[b]] + \sqrt{[[b]]^{2} + 4\cdot[[a]]}}{2} = [[answer]] \) (the positive root).
distractor_rationales: This is the negative root of the quadratic, which cannot be the limit of a sequence of square roots.
This is the value of the discriminant's square root \( \sqrt{[[b]]^{2}+4\cdot[[a]]} \), not the limit itself.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.

stem: Assuming the expression below denotes a well-defined positive real number, evaluate \[ \sqrt{[[a]] + [[b]]\sqrt{[[a]] + [[b]]\sqrt{[[a]] + [[b]]\sqrt{\cdots}}}}. \]
solution: Let \( x \) denote the value of the infinite nested radical. Because the radical repeats, \( x = \sqrt{[[a]] + [[b]]x} \), so \( x^2 - [[b]]x - [[a]] = 0 \) and \( x = \dfrac{[[b]] + \sqrt{[[b]]^{2}+4\cdot[[a]]}}{2} = [[answer]] \) (taking the positive root).
distractor_rationales: This is the negative root of the same quadratic; the nested radical is manifestly positive.
This truncates the radical after only one layer instead of using the self-similar equation.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: A sequence is defined by \( x_1 = 1 \) and \( x_{n+1} = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[d]] + x_n} \) for \( n \ge 1 \). It converges to a positive limit \( L \) satisfying \( L = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[d]] + L} \). What is \( L \)?
solution: Setting \( x_n = x_{n+1} = L \) in \( L = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[d]]+L} \) gives \( L([[d]]+L) = [[c]] \), i.e. \( L^2 + [[d]]L - [[c]] = 0 \), so \( L = \dfrac{-[[d]] + \sqrt{[[d]]^{2}+4\cdot[[c]]}}{2} = [[answer]] \) (the positive root).
distractor_rationales: This is the negative root of the same quadratic, which cannot be the limit of a sequence of positive terms.
This plugs \( x_n = 0 \) into the recursion instead of solving the fixed-point equation.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.

stem: A sequence satisfies \( x_1 = [[x1]] \) and \( x_{n+1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(x_n + \dfrac{[[c]]}{x_n}\right) \) for \( n \ge 1 \) (this is Newton's method for a square root). Given that \( x_1 > 0 \), to what limit does the sequence converge?
solution: At a fixed point \( x = \tfrac12\left(x + \tfrac{[[c]]}{x}\right) \), which simplifies to \( x = \dfrac{[[c]]}{x} \), i.e. \( x^2 = [[c]] \). Since every \( x_n>0 \), the sequence converges to \( \sqrt{[[c]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakes the average recursion for simply halving \( c \), ignoring the reciprocal term.
This is off by one under the radical from the correct limit.
This uses \( c-1 \) instead of \( c \) under the radical.
This uses \( c+1 \) instead of \( c \) under the radical.
This forgets to take the square root at all.
This is just the starting term \( x_1 \), not the limit of the sequence.
